Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[:en:April 13|April 13]]{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} [[every:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source:[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:47, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[:en:April 13|April 13]]{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} [[every:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}}

Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[:en:April 13|April 13]]{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} [[every:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}}

Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source:[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:47, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[:en:April 13|April 13]]{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[:en:Tencho|Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} [[every:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}}

Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[:en:Tencho|Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} [[every:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]][[sn:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]][[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}}

Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|qid=Q199609}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11513662]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[:en:April 13|April 13]]{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[:en:Tencho|Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|qid=Q199609}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11513662]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia. It was then deleted but preserved on everybodywiki. The imported history from everybodywiki is patchwork and contains all the contributing users, but not all the revisions are actually present. Lets hope this is sufficient attribution though especially since most of them are mine.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:28, 2 May 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=source:[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:47, 13 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = [[:en:April 13|April 13]]{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[:en:Tencho|Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[:en:Tencho|Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of [[:en:Kasuga|Kasuga]]: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a [[:en:Kokushi|Kokushi]] (provincial governor) of [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]], who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the [[:en:Tencho|Tencho]] era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, [[:en:Otomo Yoshihide|Otomo Yoshihide]], repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the [[:en:Funai Domain|Funai Domain]], [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from [[:en:Edo|Edo]] in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]] * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from [[:en:Kyushu Railway Company|Kyushu Railway Company]] [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in [[:en:Beppu|Beppu]] were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and [[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|WD=Q199609}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11513662]] Wikidata name: Kasuga-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:25, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created in the draft namespace on english wikipedia.
