Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = {{ill|April 13|en|April 13|WD=Q2498|lt=April 13}}{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture}}. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|WD=Q446420|lt=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|WD=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|WD=Q124251839|lt=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|WD=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|WD=Q446420|lt=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|WD=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|WD=Q124251839|lt=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|WD=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|WD=Q199609|1=Ōita (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = {{ill|April 13|en|April 13|WD=Q2498|lt=April 13|1=April 13}}{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|WD=Q446420|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|WD=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|WD=Q124251839|lt=Tencho|1=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|WD=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide|1=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain|1=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}} {{wikidata link|Q11513662}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Everybody linked]] In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|WD=Q199609|1=Ōita (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = {{ill|April 13|en|April 13|WD=Q2498|lt=April 13|1=April 13}}{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|WD=Q446420|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|WD=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|WD=Q124251839|lt=Tencho|1=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|WD=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide|1=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain|1=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = {{ill|April 13|en|April 13|WD=Q2498|lt=April 13|1=April 13}}{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|WD=Q446420|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|WD=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|WD=Q124251839|lt=Tencho|1=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|WD=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide|1=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain|1=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} {{everybodywiki link|Kasuga Shrine (Oita)}} [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}} {{wikidata link|Q11513662}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain|1=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|WD=Q199609|1=Ōita (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = {{ill|April 13|en|April 13|WD=Q2498|lt=April 13|1=April 13}}{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|WD=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|WD=Q446420|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|WD=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|WD=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|WD=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|WD=Q124251839|lt=Tencho|1=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|WD=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide|1=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|WD=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain|1=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|WD=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|WD=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[co:commons:Category:Kasuga-jinja (Oita)]][[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Oita|qid=Q199609|1=Ōita (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|33.24513888888889|N|131.59819444444443|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|architecture_style={{ill|nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}}}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Oita kasuga jinja.jpg |Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kasuga Shrine | image = [[File:Oita kasuga jinja.jpg|250px]] | location = 4-6-87 Seike-machi, Ōita City, Ōita Prefecture | coordinates = {{Coord|33|14|42.5|N|131|35|53.5|E|region:JP-44_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | deity = Takemikazuchi no Mikoto
Futsunushi no Mikoto
Ame no Koyane no Mikoto
Hime no Ookami | type = Former Prefectural Shrine; Beppyo Shrine | founded = Traditional date: Jōgan 2 (860) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | alternate names = | Pilgrimage = | festival = {{ill|April 13|en|April 13|qid=Q2498|lt=April 13|1=April 13}}{{Cite web|title=Regular festivals|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/event/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}} | Rituals = Chino-wa Shinji (June 30)
Shiokaki Shinji (October 19) | map = {{location map|Japan Oita Prefecture|label=Kasuga Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kasuga Shrine''' (かすがじんじゃ) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in [[:en:Ōita City|Ōita City]], {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|qid=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}. It was formerly Ranked as a prefectural shrine and is now listed as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. == Deities == Kasuga Shrine enshrines the four deities of {{ill|Kasuga|en|Kasuga|qid=Q446420|lt=Kasuga|1=Kasuga}}: * [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi no Mikoto]] * [[Futsunushi|Futsunushi no Mikoto]] * [[Ame-no-Koyane|Ame no Koyane no Mikoto]] * [[Himegami|Hime no Ookami]] == History == === Foundation === According to shrine tradition and the ''[[Bungo no Kuni-shi]]'', the shrine was founded in Jōgan 2 (860) by [[Fujiwara no Yotsuke]], a {{ill|Kokushi|en|Kokushi|qid=Q16801292|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi}} (provincial governor) of {{ill|Bungo Province|en|Bungo Province|qid=Q864148|lt=Bungo Province|1=Bungo Province}}, who enshrined the deities of the [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Taisha]] from {{ill|Yamato Province|en|Yamato Province|qid=Q907495|lt=Yamato Province|1=Yamato Province}} as his clan's guardian deities{{Cite web|title=History|url=http://www.kasuganomori.jp/history/|publisher=Kasuga Shrine|accessdate=2018-07-23}}{{cite encyclopedia|title=Kasuga Shrine|author=Yoshida Toyoharu|encyclopedia=Ōita Encyclopedia|page=137|editor=Ōita Broadcasting System Ōita Encyclopedia Editorial Office|publisher=Ōita Broadcasting System|date=1980-12-01}}. Another tradition in the ''Kasuga Shrine Abbreviation'' states that Ishikawa Toshitane enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain in Yamato during the [[:en:Tenpyo|Tenpyo]] era (729-749). Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|qid=Q124251839|lt=Tencho|1=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. However, ''Kichijo Magazine'' states that the second lord, {{ill|Otomo Yoshihide|en|Otomo Yoshihide|qid=Q1969558|lt=Otomo Yoshihide|1=Otomo Yoshihide}}, repaired the shrine in Ninji 3 (1243) and dedicated 80 koku of rice fields. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. Since then, it has been revered as the general shrine of [[Bungo Fuchu]] by successive lords. In Tensho 14 (1586), the shrine buildings were destroyed during the [[Invasion of Bungo|Satsuma invasion of Bungo]]. They were rebuilt in Keicho 8 (1603) by the first lord of the {{ill|Funai Domain|en|Funai Domain|qid=Q3034441|lt=Funai Domain|1=Funai Domain}}, [[Takenaka Shigeyoshi]]. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|qid=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in Showa 42 (1967), with the relocation ceremony held on October 18. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. In July 1968, the shrine was listed as a Beppyo Shrine by the Association of Shinto Shrines. == Shrine Forest == The shrine forest covers a total area of 26,000 m², with a wooded area of 15,600 m² and approximately 500 trees. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita City|url=http://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o177/machizukuri/toshi/1438768127009.html|publisher=Ōita City|date=2016-09-14|accessdate=2018-07-23}}, it was also designated as a special protected forest by Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and {{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|qid=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}} * Early February: Inari Shrine Hatsumode festival * February 11: [[Kigensetsu|Kigensai]] * February 17: [[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] * April 12: Spring Grand festival Eve * April 13: festival * April 29: Showa festival * May 1: Kinpira Shrine festival * June 30: Summer Great Purification ceremony and [[Chino-wa Shinji]] * July 18: Summer Grand festival Eve * July 19: Summer Grand festival and Portable Shrine Procession * September 25: Tenmangu Shrine festival * October 18: Autumn Grand festival Eve * October 19: Autumn Grand festival and Shiokaki Shinji * November 15: [[Shichi-Go-San]] festival * November 23: [[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]] * December 23: Tencho-sai * December 31: Great Purification ceremony and New Year's Eve festival == Access == * By car: About 5 minutes from {{ill|Kyushu Railway Company|en|Kyushu Railway Company|qid=Q498366|lt=Kyushu Railway Company|1=Kyushu Railway Company}} [[:en:Oita Station|Oita Station]]. * By bus: ** From Oita Station, take a bus bound for [[Oita Prefectural Library]] from Bus Stop No. 7 and get off at "Kasuga Shrine-mae". ** From Bus Stop No. 2 at Oita Station, take a bus bound for Beppu/Kunisaki, get off at "Kasugaura", and walk for 3-4 minutes{{Cite web|title=Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/sp/spots/detail/1143|publisher=Oita City Tourism Association|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. == Notes == {{Reflist|group="note"}} == References == {{Reflist|2}} == External Links == * [http://www.kasuganomori.jp Kasuga Shrine Official Website] {{Shinto-stub}} == References == {{reflist}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated page|ja|春日神社 (大分市)|version=103113718|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{Draft categories| [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] }} {{everybodywiki link|Kasuga Shrine (Oita)}} [[simple:Kasuga Shrine (Oita)]] [[ja:春日神社 (大分市)]] [[ja:Kasuga Shrine (Ōita)]] {{Commons category|Kasuga-jinja (Oita)}} {{wikidata link|Q11513662}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Kasuga shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short Additionally, it is said that Ichikawa某 enshrined the deities from Mikasa Mountain during the {{ill|Tencho|en|Tencho|qid=Q124251839|lt=Tencho|1=Tencho}} era (824-834){{Cite web|title=Seike Kasuga Shrine|url=http://kasugarengou.jp/list/2016/03/post-386.html|publisher=National Kasuga Union|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. === Medieval to Early Modern Period === In Kenkyu 7 (1196), the first lord of the [[:en:Otomo clan|Otomo clan]], [[Otomo Yoshiaki]], is said to have rebuilt the shrine. The names of the rice fields dedicated by Yoshihide were retained in the local place names until recently{{Cite book|title=Digital Book "Ōita Past and Present" Chapter 15: Around Kasugaura|author=Watanabe Katsumi|editor=Ōita Godo Shimbun|publisher=NAN-NAN Office|url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita015a1.pdf|format=PDF|date=2007-11-23}}. Yoshihide also revived the affiliated temple Takaoyama Jinguji and is said to have built Mt. Horai on the grounds{{Cite web|title=Specially Protected Forest List|url=http://www.pref.oita.jp/soshiki/16210/hogojurin.html#7|publisher=Ōita Prefecture|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. According to tradition, Shigeyoshi offered 100,000 pine seedlings to the shrine after surviving a storm on the Harima Sea while returning from {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|qid=Q215646|lt=Edo|1=Edo}} in Keicho 12 (1607). [[Hineno Yoshiaki]] revived the shrine's rituals and donated sacred treasures upon his arrival in Funai Domain in Kan'ei 11 (1634). In Shoho 2 (1645), he rebuilt Mt. Horai and constructed a shrine for the Seven Past Buddhas. === Modern Period === In Meiji 4 (1871), the shrine was Ranked as a Gōsha, and in Taisho 6 (1917), it was elevated to a prefectural shrine{{Cite web|title="Ōita Beautiful 100" Award-Winning Projects in the Architecture, Structures, and Town Development Activities Category, 24th Oita Landscape Model Award, 48. Kasuga Shrine|url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o170/machizukuri/toshi/documents/5146717c002.pdf|publisher=Ōita City|date=2017-04-01|format=PDF|accessdate=2018-07-23}}. On July 17, 1945, the shrine buildings were destroyed by incendiary bombs during the [[Bombing of Ōita]]. After World War II, the shrine buildings from the [[Onsen Shrines|Onsen Shrine]] in {{ill|Beppu|en|Beppu|qid=Q273880|lt=Beppu|1=Beppu}} were relocated to serve as temporary structures{{cite news|title=Surprising Facts about Onsen Shrine: The Former Shrine Buildings are at Kasuga Shrine|url=http://www.oita-press.co.jp/1010000000/2018/07/20/JD0057123337|newspaper=Ōita Godo Shimbun|date=2018-07-23}}. The old Onsen Shrine buildings are now used as a storage facility for portable shrines. Designated as a "Notable Tree Forest" by Ōita City on February 1, 1974{{Cite web|title=List of Notable Trees in Ōita Ōita Prefecture on March 15, 1974. == festivals == * January 1: {{ill|Saitan-sai|ja|歳旦祭|simple|User:Immanuelle/Saiten-sai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saitan-sai|qq=}} and
